summary: My family originally came from northern Switzerland, which is part of our heritage along with southern Germany. The Kistler family roots trace back to that region before migrating to America in the early 18th century. It’s interesting to think about how far back our origins go, starting from that part of Europe.

The Kistler family, which is my family, originally came from southern Germany before coming to America around 1723. They first settled in New York and then moved down to Pennsylvania. Germany is a key part of our family history and identity, even though we’ve been in the U.S. for many generations.

The Kistler family is my family, and we have a long history going back to Germany and Switzerland. We came to America around 1723 and settled first in New York before moving to Pennsylvania. I’ve visited Kistler Valley in Pennsylvania twice, where many Kistlers still live, and it’s always felt like coming home. The family has deep roots in farming and community life there.

On my maternal side, my grandmother was English, so that part of my heritage comes from England. While the Kistlers came from Germany and Switzerland, my mother’s family adds that English connection. It’s a nice blend of European backgrounds in my family history.

When the Kistler family first came to America around 1723, they settled in New York before eventually moving down to Pennsylvania. I was working in New York around 1940 when I first visited Kistler Valley in Pennsylvania. I even had a New York license plate on my car during that visit, which made the local Kistlers a bit suspicious at first.

Pennsylvania is where the Kistler family finally established themselves after migrating from New York. There’s a beautiful place called Kistler Valley outside of Allentown, Pennsylvania, which is rich in farming and dairy country. I’ve been back there twice, once around 1940 and again in the early 1980s, and it always felt like visiting home.

Kistler Valley in Pennsylvania is a special place for me because it’s where many Kistlers settled and farmed. I visited there twice, once in 1940 and again around 1981 or 1982. The valley is beautiful and green, with a church and a cemetery full of Kistler stones. Seeing signs like 'William A. Kistler Garage' and the old stone houses built by Samuel Kistler made me feel deeply connected to my family history.

Kistler Valley is located outside of Allentown, Pennsylvania, which situates my family’s roots near that area. The valley is known for its farming and dairy country, and it’s a beautiful green landscape. Visiting near Allentown gave me a chance to reconnect with the Kistlers still living there and explore the history of our family homes.

I traveled from Ohio down into Pennsylvania with Lelah in the early 1980s to visit Kistler Valley. That trip was memorable because we met some local Kistlers quilting and saw signs of my family’s legacy like the William A. Kistler Garage. Ohio was the starting point for that journey back to my family’s roots.

Samuel Kistler was an ancestor who built a stone house in Kistler Valley in 1812, according to the cornerstone we found. I even have an old Bible that belonged to a Samuel Kistler, though I’m not sure if it’s the same one. Finding his house and the Bible made me feel like I had struck gold in connecting with my family’s past.

William A. Kistler was my father’s name, and seeing a sign for 'William A. Kistler Garage' in Kistler Valley was a touching moment for me. It made me feel at home and connected to the family history there. That sign was one of the highlights of my visit in the early 1980s.
